examples.en: I am going to the market.
He is at home.

examples.kp: Sali ku ning prutas king tindahan.
examples.en: I will buy fruit at the store.

examples.kp: Magkanu ya ing libro king tindahan?
examples.en: How much is the book at the store?

entries.notes: The primary locative/oblique particle in Kapampangan, equivalent to Tagalog 'sa'. Marks location ('in/at'), direction ('to/into'), recipient ('for'), and oblique nouns generally. It is the oblique counterpart of 'ing' (absolutive) and 'ning' (genitive). Extremely high frequency — appears in virtually every sentence with a locative complement. Confidence: very high; attested extensively in grammars (Bergaño tradition) and all modern sources.

examples.en: I have money to buy (something).

examples.kp: Kailangan ku ning gamut para king sakit ku.
examples.en: I need medicine for my illness.

examples.kp: Atin ku pung payung para king uran.
examples.en: I have an umbrella for the rain.

entries.notes: Spanish loanword (para) fully integrated into Kapampangan. Used to mark purpose ('in order to') or beneficiary ('for'). Often precedes king + noun phrase. Confidence: very high.

examples.en: Good morning (polite).
Good evening, grandfather (respectful).
Please help me.

examples.kp: Makilabas ku pu, munta ku na.
examples.en: Excuse me, I am leaving now.

entries.notes: Politeness/respect enclitic, equivalent to Tagalog 'po'. Used when addressing elders, strangers, or people of higher status. Often appears fused as 'pung' (pu + ng ligature) before vowel-initial words: e.g., 'Atin ku pung payung'. Confidence: very high; widely attested.

examples.en: I am still hungry.
My younger sibling is still little.
We still have (some).
Go to the store again / also.

entries.notes: Aspectual/modal enclitic particle. Indicates continuation of a state ('still'), incompleteness ('yet'), or addition ('also/more'). Cognate with and functions similarly to Tagalog 'pa'. Confidence: very high.

examples.kp: Nung ati kang sakit, punta ka king doktor.
examples.en: If you are sick, go to the doctor.
There are many stars when it is night.
I am sad when you leave.

entries.notes: Conditional/temporal subordinating conjunction. Equivalent to Tagalog 'kung' (if) or 'kapag/pag' (when). Used for both hypothetical conditions and habitual/temporal clauses. Confidence: very high; attested in existing pack sentences.

examples.kp: Malungkut ing anak uling wala ne ing asawa na.
examples.en: The child is sad because his parent is gone.

examples.kp: Marine ya uling binura ne ing sulat na.
examples.en: She is embarrassed because her letter was erased.

examples.kp: Marirya ya uling dakal a lugud ku para king anak.
examples.en: She is touched because I have great love for the child.

entries.notes: Causal subordinating conjunction meaning 'because/since'. Equivalent to Tagalog 'kasi' or 'dahil'. Introduces the reason or cause clause. Confidence: very high; directly attested in existing pack sentences.

examples.en: I want to go to the store.
I want to go to the mountain.
What do you want?
I want coffee.

entries.notes: Stative verb/modal meaning 'to want, to like'. Takes the genitive enclitic ku (I) or mu (you) when the wanter is subject. Very commonly followed by an infinitive verb phrase. Attested in existing pack sentences and the Wikipedia Kapampangan article ('Isanu deti ya ing bisa ka?'). Confidence: very high.

examples.en: What is your name?
Your cooking is delicious.
Your bag is big.

examples.kp: Malyari mu lang palitan dening pera ku?
examples.en: Can you exchange my money for me?

entries.notes: 2nd-person singular genitive/non-subject (ng-set) enclitic pronoun. Marks possession ('your') or the actor of a non-topic action ('by you'). Equivalent to Tagalog 'mo'. Not to be confused with the particle 'mu' meaning 'only/just', which is a separate homophone sometimes noted in older Kapampangan texts but rare in modern speech. Confidence: very high.

examples.en: I will give the book to him/her.
I will take the book.
I will sweep the path.
OK, I will take it.

entries.notes: Portmanteau pronoun combining 1st-singular actor (ku) and 3rd-singular object (ya). The form 'ke' is the reduced/fused enclitic that appears when both a 1sg actor and a 3sg patient are expressed together, analogous to Tagalog 'ko' (actor) + 'siya/ito' context. Some analyses derive it as ku + ya → ke by vowel coalescence. Listed in Kapampangan grammar references as a lexicalized enclitic cluster. Confidence: high — directly attested in existing pack sentences.

examples.kp: Dakal a pagmaragul king puso ku para keka.
examples.en: There is great pride in my heart for you.
I miss you!
The doctor will help you.
Good morning to you (polite).

entries.notes: Oblique (sa-set) 2nd-person singular pronoun. Equivalent to Tagalog 'sa iyo'. Used as indirect object ('to you'), recipient ('for you'), or other oblique roles. Contrast with absolutive 'ka' (you, topic) and genitive 'mu' (your). Confidence: very high; widely attested.

examples.en: Tell me.

examples.kp: Mayap ya ing Ingkung ku kaku.
examples.en: My grandfather is good to me.

examples.kp: Matula ku king lugud mu para kaku.
examples.en: I am happy about your love for me.
My aunt is the one who called me.

entries.notes: Oblique (sa-set) 1st-person singular pronoun. Equivalent to Tagalog 'sa akin'. Also sometimes written or pronounced 'kanaku'. Used as indirect object ('to me'), recipient ('for me'), or other oblique roles. Confidence: very high; directly attested in existing pack sentences.

examples.en: I will give the book to him/her.
I am writing a letter to him/her.
I have great love for her.

entries.notes: Oblique (sa-set) 3rd-person singular pronoun. Equivalent to Tagalog 'sa kanya'. Also found as 'keya' in some dialect/register variation, but 'kaniya' is the standard modern form. Used as indirect object or other oblique role. Confidence: very high; attested in existing pack sentences.

examples.kp: Ibie ke ing susi kang Carmen.
examples.en: I will give the key to Carmen.
I am writing a letter to Father.

examples.kp: Ibiye mu kang Pedro ing kwalta.
examples.en: Give the money to Pedro.

entries.notes: Oblique personal article used before proper names (personal names, kinship terms used as names). Equivalent to Tagalog 'kay'. Marks the oblique (indirect object, beneficiary, location) role of a named person. Contrast with 'king' (oblique for common nouns) and 'kang' as a contraction of 'ka + ng' ligature (a separate use). Confidence: high.

examples.kp: Atin libre a pagkain kareng anak.
examples.en: There is free food for the children.
I am full from the rice (among/of the rice).

examples.kp: Ibie me kareng tau ing pagkain.
examples.en: Give the food to the people.

entries.notes: Oblique (sa-set) plural article. Equivalent to Tagalog 'sa mga'. Used when the oblique noun phrase is plural and is a common noun. Corresponds to the absolutive plural 'reng/ding'. Confidence: high; attested in existing pack sentences.

examples.en: There is fire here, let us be careful.
I have money to buy (something).

examples.kp: Atin ku pung payung para king uran.
examples.en: I have an umbrella for the rain.
There are many stones in the river.

entries.notes: Existential verb/particle meaning 'there is/are' or 'to have'. Equivalent to Tagalog 'mayroon/meron'. When combined with a pronoun enclitic (e.g., atin ku = I have), it expresses possession. Negated by 'ala' (there is not). Confidence: very high; extensively attested.

examples.en: He is at home.

examples.kp: Atyu ya king lalam ning lamesa.
examples.en: It is under the table.

examples.kp: Atyu ya ing kotsi king arap ning bale.
examples.en: The car is in front of the house.
The food is on the table.

entries.notes: Locative existential verb/predicate. While 'atin' asserts existence ('there is'), 'atyu' specifies location ('is located at/in'). Equivalent to Tagalog 'nandoon/narito/nasa'. Requires a locative complement marked by 'king'. Confidence: very high; extensively attested in existing packs.

examples.en: How do you do this?
This is too expensive.
What is this?
This is delicious.

entries.notes: Proximal demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this' (near the speaker). Equivalent to Tagalog 'ito'. Functions as a noun substitute. Attested in Wikipedia ('Makananu iti gawan?') and widely in other sources. Contrast with 'iyan' (that, near addressee) and 'ita' (that, far from both). Confidence: very high.

examples.en: Whose bag is this?
These fruits are delicious.
This dress is beautiful.

entries.notes: Proximal demonstrative, closely related to 'iti'. 'Ini' typically functions as a post-nominal modifier ('the bag ini' = 'this bag'), while 'iti' is more often used as a sentence-level predicative or object pronoun. The distinction in modern Kapampangan is not always sharp; both translate to 'this'. Attested in existing pack sentences ('Kaninu ya ing bag ini?'). Confidence: high.

examples.kp: Isanu deti ya ing bisa ka?
examples.en: Which of these do you want?
These fruits are delicious.
Wait for me here (at these).
These fruits are many.

entries.notes: Proximal plural demonstrative pronoun meaning 'these'. Equivalent to Tagalog 'mga ito'. The plural counterpart of 'iti/ini'. Attested in Wikipedia ('Isanu deti ya ing bisa ka?') and in existing pack sentences. Confidence: very high.

lessons.title: Key Particles: king, para, pu, pa, nung, uling, bisa
lessons.intro: Kapampangan has a set of particles and conjunctions that appear in almost every sentence. The locative particle 'king' (= Tagalog 'sa') is the most common word in the language — it marks location, direction, and oblique nouns. 'Para' (for/in order to) marks purpose. 'Pu' adds politeness when speaking to elders. 'Pa' means 'still/yet/more'. 'Nung' introduces 'if/when' clauses, and 'uling' means 'because'. 'Bisa' is the main verb for wanting or liking something.

exercises.prompt: Which word means "to want" or "to like"?
What does "pu" signal in a sentence?

accepted: i am going to the market
i am going to the market.
i will go to the market
i will go to the market.

exercises.prompt: Nung ati kang sakit, punta ka king doktor.
accepted: if you are sick, go to the doctor
if you are sick, go to the doctor.
when you are sick, go to the doctor
when you are sick, go to the doctor.

lessons.title: Oblique Pronouns: mu, ke, keka, kaku, kaniya, kang, kareng
lessons.theme: The sa-set (oblique) pronouns and genitive enclitic
lessons.intro: Kapampangan has a full set of oblique pronouns — equivalents of Tagalog 'sa akin, sa iyo, sa kanya'. These mark indirect objects, beneficiaries, and other non-topic roles. 'Mu' is the 2nd-singular genitive enclitic meaning 'your' or 'by you'. 'Ke' is a portmanteau combining I + it/him/her into a single enclitic. 'Keka' means 'to/for you', 'kaku' means 'to/for me', and 'kaniya' means 'to/for him or her'. For proper names, 'kang' is used (= Tagalog 'kay'). For plural nouns, 'kareng' is the oblique plural article.

exercises.prompt: How do you say "to you / for you" (singular) in Kapampangan?

accepted: what is your name
what is your name?
what is your name?.
i will give the book to him
i will give the book to him.
i will give the book to her
i will give the book to her.

exercises.prompt: Ibie ke ing susi ____ Carmen. (to — before a name)

lessons.title: Existentials & Demonstratives: atin, atyu, iti, ini, deti
lessons.intro: Two verbs cover existence and location in Kapampangan. 'Atin' (there is/are; to have) is the general existential — equivalent to Tagalog 'mayroon'. 'Atyu' (is located at) is the locative existential that always pairs with the particle 'king' to specify where. For pointing out things nearby, Kapampangan uses three demonstratives: 'iti' (this — used as a pronoun), 'ini' (this — used as a modifier after the noun), and 'deti' (these — plural). These words let learners point at and identify objects in conversation.

accepted: there is fire here, let us be careful
there is fire here, let us be careful.
there is fire here, we must be careful
there is fire here, we must be careful.

exercises.prompt: Atyu ya king lalam ning lamesa.
accepted: it is under the table
it is under the table.
he is under the table
he is under the table.

exercises.prompt: Kaninu ya ing bag ____? (this — modifier form)
